---
title: "Day 3 (Thursday): Advanced Models, Metrics, and Explainability"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../assets/css/slides-theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    incremental: true
    scrollable: true
    slide-level: 2
---

## Learning objectives

- Compare random forests, XGBoost, and neural networks
- Handle imbalance and missingness in pipelines
- Select metrics based on problem objectives
- Introduce variable importance and SHAP intuition

## Navigation

- [Teaching Hub](../index.qmd)
- [Curriculum Outline](../curriculum-outline.qmd)
- [Module Library](../modules/index.qmd)
- [Back: Tuesday deck](day-02-tuesday.qmd)

## Model progression

- Random forests: robust defaults, low tuning burden
- XGBoost: strong tabular performance with careful tuning
- Neural networks: flexible but data and tuning hungry

## Data preparation at this stage

- Missing data strategy should live in the recipe
- Avoid test leakage when imputing
- Keep transformations reproducible inside workflows

## Same tidymodels template (swap model spec)

Thursday uses the **same** `_includes/tidymodels-pipeline.qmd` as Tuesday: keep **`folds`**, **`metric_set(...)`**, and the **`tune_grid` → `select_best` → `finalize_workflow` → `fit`** rhythm fixed; change **`mod_spec`** (e.g. `rand_forest` + `ranger`, `boost_tree` + `xgboost`, `mlp` + `nnet`) and any **recipe** steps (missingness, imbalance, feature engineering).

- **Step-by-step code + figures** (tuning plot, `vip`, `rpart.plot`, ROC): see **Tuesday** slides *Shared pipeline template* and [Module 04: Tidymodels Pipeline](../modules/module-04-pipeline.qmd). **Same penguins table** as [dataset-penguins](../dataset-penguins.qmd) and [penguins-classification.Rmd](../notebooks/penguins-classification.Rmd).

## Class imbalance (tie-in: Palmer Penguins)

- **Three species** on the full dataset are **not** as skewed as many clinical endpoints — use them to discuss **what imbalance would look like**, then contrast with **gene** disease prevalence in the simulation notebooks.
- **Adelie vs Gentoo** (Tuesday pipeline slice) is closer to balanced; still practice **sensitivity/specificity** from `metric_set` and ask when **accuracy** misleads.
- Accuracy can be misleading when classes are skewed
- Use class weights, resampling, or threshold tuning
- Evaluate with precision/recall-centric metrics when needed

## Demo: class imbalance intuition

- Optional: show one confusion matrix at two prevalence levels (board or a quick R snippet in exercises).

## Metrics toolbox

- Accuracy: useful only with balanced classes and symmetric costs
- Precision/recall/F1: useful when false positives and false negatives are asymmetric
- ROC-AUC: ranking quality across thresholds
- PR-AUC: better signal for rare positive class
- Calibration: do probabilities match observed frequencies?

## What to optimize and when

- Screening: prioritize recall
- Confirmatory test: prioritize precision
- Ranking pipelines: monitor ROC-AUC plus calibration
- Operational systems: include business or clinical cost context

## Variable importance

- Model-specific importance (e.g., split gain in trees)
- Permutation importance as model-agnostic baseline
- Correlated features can distort importance rankings

## Explainability beyond a single number

- **Global:** which features matter on average across the dataset?
- **Local:** why did *this* patient receive *this* score?
- **Faithfulness:** an explanation is only as trustworthy as the model’s fit and the data generating process you condition on.

## SHAP: Shapley values for ML

- SHAP (**SH**apley **A**dditive ex**P**lanations) builds on cooperative game theory: each feature is a “player” contributing to the prediction surplus over a baseline (expected model output).
- For an additive explanation model \(\hat f(x) \approx \phi_0 + \sum_j \phi_j(x)\), SHAP values \(\phi_j\) are constructed so they **sum to the deviation** from the baseline: \(\sum_j \phi_j = \hat f(x) - \phi_0\).
- Different **SHAP formulations** correspond to different ways of averaging over feature coalitions (interventional vs conditional on correlations); this matters when predictors are dependent.

## TreeSHAP vs KernelSHAP (practical angle)

- **TreeSHAP** exploits tree structure (random forests, GBMs) for exact or efficient Shapley-style attributions under the model’s native partition of the input space—fast at scale for tree ensembles.
- **KernelSHAP** is model-agnostic: it approximates Shapley values by sampling coalitions and fitting a weighted linear model around the prediction—flexible but slower and more noise-sensitive, especially in high dimensions.
- Rule of thumb: use **TreeSHAP** for `xgboost` / `ranger` / `rpart`; consider **KernelSHAP** (or other explainers) when you must explain arbitrary black-box APIs.

## Reading SHAP plots

- **Waterfall / force:** shows signed contributions \(\phi_j\) stacking from baseline to final log-odds or score for one observation—best for teaching “where the prediction came from.”
- **Summary beeswarm / bar:** orders features by mean \(|\phi_j|\) and shows distribution of impacts across individuals—highlights global drivers while preserving sign information.
- **Dependence:** plots \(\phi_j\) vs feature value \(x_j\) to reveal interactions (color by a second feature) or nonlinear leverage.

## SHAP limitations (say this out loud)

- **Not causal:** large SHAP mass on a variable does not prove manipulation of that variable would change outcomes in the real world.
- **Correlated inputs:** attribution can be split arbitrarily between twins; use domain knowledge, grouped explanations, or regularized surrogate models.
- **Adversarial models:** a model can hide dependence from naive explainers; treat SHAP as a *sanity check*, not a proof of safety.
- **Baseline choice:** interventional SHAP depends on the reference distribution; changing the baseline shifts all \(\phi_j\).

## Model-agnostic baselines to pair with SHAP

- **Permutation importance:** shuffle one column in validation data and measure drop in score—simple, unstable with collinearity, but intuitive.
- **Partial dependence / accumulated local effects:** trace average prediction vs one or two features—complements local SHAP by showing global curvature.
- **Calibration plots:** check whether predicted probabilities line up with observed frequencies before trusting SHAP on probability space.

## Nested resampling when tuning gets serious

- **Problem:** if you tune on the same outer folds you use for final comparison, optimistic bias creeps in.
- **Pattern:** outer `vfold_cv` for performance; inside each analysis fold, inner CV or bootstrap for hyperparameter selection; aggregate outer metrics only.
- **tidymodels:** implement nested splits with **composed `rsample` objects** (inner resampling inside each `analysis()` fold of an outer `vfold_cv`); see official case studies and optional `finetune` for racing methods.
- Teaching tip: draw the **two-layer diagram** on the board once; students reuse it for every advanced model day.

## Fairness and subgroup checks (light touch)

- Once metrics are chosen, report them **overall and by subgroup** (sex, site, batch) when those fields exist.
- Watch for **large accuracy gaps** driven by prevalence differences rather than meaningful model quality.
- Pair with curriculum idea: separate half-day on causal vs associational claims before students ship SHAP plots to stakeholders.

## Demo: SHAP intuition

- Optional: one hand-drawn “waterfall” of signed contributions for a toy two-feature model in class.

## Final takeaway

- Strong modeling requires both algorithm choice and evaluation design
- Metrics and explainability must match the deployment objective
- Exercises are handled separately
